metadata.title: Stop using your own Claude at work.

beats.narration_text: this is Liam, in for Bear. Nine out of ten companies have employees using personal AI accounts at work — without telling IT. Fifty-seven percent have typed sensitive information into them at least once. And you probably have too. The real question is the one nobody wants to answer out loud: can your boss actually fire you for that? The short answer is yes. Here's how to keep using your personal Claude without ending your career.

props.segment: Stop Using Your Own Claude At Work
props.command: Can I actually get fired for using my personal Claude account at work?

beats.narration_text: The Samsung case matters because it's public and it's damning. April 2023. Engineers were given permission to use ChatGPT at work. Within twenty days, they had leaked internal semiconductor source code three separate times — pasted into ChatGPT to check bugs, to fix defects, and one time they uploaded a recording of an internal meeting. Samsung banned ChatGPT company-wide and opened disciplinary investigations. That's the ceiling on what goes wrong.

props.artifactHeading: What happens when it goes wrong
artifactLines: Engineers permitted to use ChatGPT → leaked source code 3 times in 20 days.
Semiconductor code, defect code, internal meeting recording — all pasted in.
Result: company-wide ChatGPT ban + disciplinary investigations.
Personal AI = outside party. That's what the NDA you signed covers.

beats.narration_text: Here's the mechanism. On personal Claude plans — Free, Pro, Max — Anthropic's terms allow your chats and coding sessions to be used to improve the model by default. Leave that on, and they can keep your conversations for up to five years. Every piece of confidential company information you paste in sits in that pool. The fix is one toggle, and you need to flip it before the next conversation you have.

shot.slotNote: SLOT — fill media/B02.png (screenshot of Claude Settings → Privacy → 'Help improve our AI models' toggle being turned off)

beats.narration_text: For Claude: Settings → Privacy → turn off Help improve our AI models. For ChatGPT: Settings → Data controls → turn off Improve the model for everyone. Grok: profile, settings, data controls, turn off everything. Gemini is harder — you navigate to myactivity.google.com and turn off the Gemini activity toggle directly. Important caveat One additional guide covers: the opt-out only applies going forward. Data already in a training run can't be pulled back. Flip the switch now, not after your next session.

props.artifactTitle: Turn off training — all four AIs
artifactLines: Claude: Settings → Privacy → 'Help improve our AI models' → off.
ChatGPT: Settings → Data controls → 'Improve the model for everyone' → off.
Grok: profile → settings → data controls → turn off everything.
Gemini: myactivity.google.com → Gemini activity → off (no shortcut).

beats.narration_text: the not a lawyer — he's clear about that. But the cases are public. Pasting confidential company information into a personal AI account can be framed three ways by a lawyer: breach of the NDA you signed, because the AI company is an outside party. Violation of data protection laws, depending on jurisdiction. Breach of IT security policy. All three have precedent. The risk isn't hypothetical. It's already happened, and companies have opened investigations over it.

props.artifactTitle: Three legal angles your lawyer will use
artifactLines: NDA breach: AI company = outside party. Your contract likely covers this.
Data protection violation: jurisdiction-dependent, but real exposure.
IT security policy breach: even if no NDA clause, the policy likely does.
None of these require malicious intent. A paste is enough.

beats.narration_text: The practical solution, if your company won't pay for an enterprise AI account: keep your personal account, but run a clean-room policy for yourself. Never paste client names, internal financials, source code, or meeting recordings. Anonymize before you paste — replace proper nouns with placeholders. And turn off training, as covered. If you're the one managing risk for a team, the advice is the same: flag the legal exposure and advocate for an enterprise plan. Enterprise plans don't train on your data at all.

shot.slotNote: SLOT — fill media/B05.png (screenshot showing Claude chat with placeholder names like [CLIENT_NAME] or [COMPANY], demonstrating clean-room pasting practice)

beats.narration_text: Your turn. Run this audit prompt on your own Claude settings right now — takes two minutes.

props.segment: Stop Using Your Own Claude At Work
props.command: I use my personal Claude account at work. What data-hygiene rules should I follow so I don't violate my NDA or company policy? Give me a short checklist I can print and keep at my desk.

beats.narration_text: Turn off training. Anonymize what you paste. Advocate for enterprise. That's the whole play.

props.title: Stop using your own Claude at work.
props.subline: Flip the toggle. Keep your job.
